summary: Jürgen Schmidhuber discusses his lifelong ambition to build a superhuman AI so he can retire, and his perspective on the history of artificial intelligence. He emphasizes that deep learning concepts date back much further than commonly acknowledged (to 1965 and even 1800) and argues that there is no meaningful distinction between symbolic and sub-symbolic AI. He also outlines his early work on meta-learning and artificial curiosity, which foreshadowed modern generative adversarial networks. Jürgen Schmidhuber discusses his pioneering work in neural networks, arguing that many foundational AI concepts (LSTMs, Transformers, GANs, distillation, pre-training) originated in his lab in the early 1990s. He emphasizes that true AGI requires physical robotics, dismisses the 'AI winter' as an American myth, and attributes the recent dominance of Transformers over RNNs primarily to the brute-force parallelization enabled by cheap GPU compute. Jürgen Schmidhuber discusses the origins of foundational AI concepts developed in his lab during the 1990s and 2000s, including adversarial networks, artificial curiosity, meta-learning, and reinforcement learning with memory. He attributes the delayed adoption of these ideas to the prohibitive cost of compute at the time and emphasizes the importance of hierarchical planning and intrinsic motivation for advanced AI.

principles.rationale: Following Occam's razor, the simplest explanation for the universe, lacking any physical evidence to the contrary, is that it is being computed. Physics is essentially the set of all possible computations generating all possible universes.
principles.source_quote: So there's no physical evidence for that. So as Arkham's razor guys, as scientific um guys, we go for the simplest explanation which is that everything is computable.

principles.statement: There is no boundary between symbolic and sub-symbolic AI.
principles.rationale: A recurrent neural network can build NAND gates, meaning it can emulate a CPU and run any symbolic computation or theorem proving that a standard computer can.
principles.source_quote: For me, there is no difference between symbolic AI and sub symbolic AI because I cannot see the boundary between them. The boundary is totally blurred.

principles.statement: Humans are not the final stage of evolution.
principles.rationale: The universe started with simple initial conditions and trends toward increasing complexity. A new kind of intelligence is emerging that will surpass human intelligence.
principles.source_quote: it became clear to me that humans um cannot be the crown of creation and that there's something about to happen which will lead to a higher step of the evolution of the universe which wants to become more and more complex.

principles.statement: True AGI requires mastery of the physical world.
principles.rationale: Superhuman AI exists in virtual environments (behind screens), but hardware and robotics progress much slower than compute power. Until an AI can perform physical tasks like a plumber, it is not a true AGI.
principles.source_quote: AGI without mastery of the real world is no AGI. AGI without real robots in the real world is no AGI.

principles.statement: Recurrent Neural Networks are general-purpose computers.
principles.rationale: Because RNNs have recurrent connections that allow them to memorize previously seen data, they can emulate a network of NAND gates, meaning they can theoretically run any program a standard CPU can.
principles.source_quote: a recurrent network is much more powerful than a feed forward network because it's basically a generalpurpose computer in the same way that your laptop is a general purpose computer

principles.rationale: If a system can accurately predict the next token in a sequence, it already possesses that information. By ignoring predictable elements and focusing only on unpredictable events, a system can greatly compress sequences.
principles.source_quote: if you can predict the next one it means your system already knows um this information contained in the next one and you can ignore it... So you have to focus only on the unpredictable events in the sequence which means... you can greatly um compress the sequence

principles.statement: The delayed adoption of early AI breakthroughs was purely a function of compute costs.
principles.rationale: Concepts like artificial curiosity and meta-learning were theoretically sound in the 1990s, but compute was 10 million times more expensive, making them impractical to scale until hardware caught up.
principles.source_quote: The answer is always the same because of um the expensiveness of computing power because um back then you know um 1990 compute was about 10 million times more expensive.

principles.rationale: Markovian models, which assume the current input contains all necessary information, work for board games but fail in real-world environments where important variables become temporarily hidden and must be remembered.
principles.source_quote: it is not good enough for the real world where you know where you're playing video games for example and now your enemy vanished behind a boulder and now you have to memorize that he's behind the boulder

principles.statement: Advanced AI agents need intrinsic reward to drive discovery.
principles.rationale: Beyond just satisfying external goals (like finding energy), an agent needs an internal motivation to explore unknown data, achieved by rewarding the controller for surprising its own internal world model.
principles.source_quote: the intrinsic reward uh the the joy of a scientist for discovering stuff that it didn't know before.

frameworks.name: Artificial Curiosity (Adversarial Learning)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When building an autonomous agent that needs to explore its environment and build a model of the world from scratch.
steps: Create a 'world model' network that learns to predict the environmental inputs given past actions and observations.
Create a 'controller' network that generates actions to interact with the environment.
Define the world model's error (e.g., mean squared error) based on its prediction accuracy.
Reward the controller proportionally to the error of the world model, forcing them to fight each other.
Allow the controller to invent new experiments (action sequences) that lead to surprising data where the world model is still bad and can learn.
frameworks.source_quote: So I said okay let's just give the controller a reward that is proportional to the error of the model network. So suddenly the controller and the model of the world are fighting each other.

frameworks.name: Self-Referential Meta-Learning
frameworks.when_to_apply: When you want a system to not just learn a task, but learn how to learn, improving its own learning algorithm over time.
steps: Establish a domain level where facts, data, and the user-given evaluation function reside.
Create a meta-level program that generates changes in the domain-level programs.
Create a meta-meta-level program that learns to improve the meta-level program.
Apply evolutionary pressure so the system iteratively improves its own learning methods.
frameworks.source_quote: you also evolve the um the learning method itself, the the program that is generating the changes uh in the other programs... And then on top of that, a third level where you have a meta meta program that learns to improve the meta level

frameworks.name: Fast Weight Programmers (Linear Transformers)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When you need a neural network architecture that can rapidly adapt to incoming sequences while scaling linearly with compute, rather than quadratically.
steps: Train a 'slow network' via gradient descent to generate two patterns: a 'key' and a 'value'.
Pass the key to the input side and the value to the output side of a separate 'fast network'.
Rapidly change the weights of the fast network using the outer product of the key and value.
Apply the fast network to incoming queries to minimize the error function (e.g., predicting the next token).
frameworks.source_quote: there are two networks. There's a slow network and there's a fast network and the slow network how does it program the weights of the fast network which is then being applied to queries as they are coming in

frameworks.name: Artificial Curiosity
frameworks.when_to_apply: When training an agent to autonomously explore and learn about an unknown, complex environment.
steps: Deploy a controller network to generate a sequence of actions in the environment.
Use a prediction machine (world model) to predict the consequences of those actions.
Calculate the error of the prediction machine.
Use the sum of the prediction errors as the reward for the controller, motivating it to find data that surprises the world model.
frameworks.source_quote: the controller is maximizing the same thing that the prediction machine is minimizing.

frameworks.name: Learning to Think (Hierarchical Planning)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When an agent needs to solve a novel task efficiently by leveraging a pre-trained world model, rather than planning every micro-action.
steps: Train a large world model on vast amounts of data (e.g., video data) to understand the environment.
Give a controller network extra connections to send prompt sequences into the hidden units of the world model.
Allow the controller to receive answers back and learn to interpret them.
Train the controller on a sequence of tasks so it learns to address and extract high-level, important information from the world model to accelerate its own learning.
frameworks.source_quote: you just give the controller which is the learning machine... extra connections such that it can send prompt sequences into the into the world model into the hidden units of the world model and then something will come back

mental_models.name: The Artificial Scientist
mental_models.description: Viewing an AI not as a passive pattern recognizer, but as an active scientist that self-invents experiments to generate surprising data, thereby improving its own world model over time.
mental_models.example: A two-network system where a controller generates actions to maximize the prediction error of a world model, driving the system to constantly seek out new, unlearned parts of its environment.

mental_models.name: Physics as Computation
mental_models.description: The lens that physics is simply the sum of all mathematics, specifically the set of all possible computations which generate all kinds of logically possible universes.
mental_models.example: Reasoning that since there is no physical evidence our universe is incomputable, we can assume it is being computed optimally alongside all other logically possible universes (like video games with slightly different gravity values).

mental_models.name: Distillation (Collapsing Neural Networks)
mental_models.description: A method of transferring knowledge where a higher-level, complex network learns a task, and a lower-level, simpler network learns to imitate the higher level's output, absorbing its knowledge.
mental_models.example: Used in 1991 to collapse hierarchical recurrent networks into a single network, and cited as the central mechanism behind modern efficient models like DeepSeek.

mental_models.name: Predictive Coding
mental_models.description: A learning mechanism where a system constantly tries to predict the next input. Predictable inputs are ignored, and learning focuses entirely on the unpredictable, novel information.
mental_models.example: Pre-training language models by predicting the next token in a sequence, which forces the network to compress the data by understanding its underlying patterns.

mental_models.name: The Joy of a Scientist
mental_models.description: Viewing an AI's intrinsic motivation as analogous to a scientist's drive to discover unknown things by conducting experiments that surprise their current understanding of the world.
mental_models.example: A robot exploring a room not just to find its charging station (external reward), but actively seeking out new obstacles or dynamics to improve its internal prediction model of the room.

mental_models.name: Self-Referential Weight Matrix
mental_models.description: A neural network that acts as a general-purpose computer capable of running an arbitrary, differentiable weight-change algorithm on itself to learn how to learn.
mental_models.example: A meta-learning LSTM that learns a new learning algorithm which ends up being 50 times faster than standard gradient descent.

heuristics: In all genres of media (comics, novels, science fiction), 99% is trash and 1% is really interesting and good stuff.
Every five years, computing gets 10 times cheaper.
Computing power gets 10 times cheaper every five years.
If you can predict the next thing, you can ignore it.
Every 5 years computer is getting roughly 10 times cheaper.

signature_quotes.text: I want to build this thing that is much smarter than myself and then I'm done.
signature_quotes.context: Describing his lifelong ambition that started in his teenage years.

signature_quotes.text: For me, there is no difference between symbolic AI and sub symbolic AI because I cannot see the boundary between them.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how neural networks can emulate logic gates and CPUs.

signature_quotes.text: As Arkham's razor guys, as scientific um guys, we go for the simplest explanation which is that everything is computable.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the nature of the universe and physics.

signature_quotes.text: Modern AI is built all on these deep deep neural networks and basically it's built on 60 year old stuff.
signature_quotes.context: Highlighting that the foundations of deep learning were laid in 1965 in Ukraine.

signature_quotes.text: AGI without mastery of the real world is no AGI. AGI without real robots in the real world is no AGI.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing why claims that AGI is 'near' are premature due to the lack of progress in physical robotics.

signature_quotes.text: I never really bought the story of the neural network winter. I think that was more like an American thing.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on the 1970s and 1980s, noting that AI funding and research continued robustly in Europe and Japan.

signature_quotes.text: All the basic insights that are now driving modern AI they are all rooted in the previous millennium.
signature_quotes.context: Summarizing how concepts like backpropagation, LSTMs, CNNs, and Transformers were invented decades before their widespread commercial adoption.

signature_quotes.text: In the real world everything that we use is a finite state automaton.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining that true Turing completeness is a theoretical construct requiring infinite storage, which doesn't exist in the physical universe.

signature_quotes.text: The answer is always the same because of um the expensiveness of computing power because um back then you know um 1990 compute was about 10 million times more expensive.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why his early 1990s AI breakthroughs took decades to be adopted.

signature_quotes.text: The controller is maximizing the same thing that the prediction machine is minimizing.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the core mechanism of artificial curiosity and adversarial networks.

signature_quotes.text: Some people say Apple came late to the AI party, but no, they started early on.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing how Apple hired researchers from his lab around 2010/2011.

anti_patterns.description: Ignoring the true historical pioneers of deep learning and neural networks.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It leads to a distorted view of AI history, where modern researchers fail to cite foundational work (like Ivakhnenko and Lapa's 1965 deep learning work or Gauss's 1800 neural network foundations), often due to Cold War biases or poor literature review.

anti_patterns.description: Treating symbolic AI and sub-symbolic (neural network) AI as fundamentally different or opposed fields.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores the mathematical reality that neural networks can emulate logic gates (NAND gates) and CPUs, making the boundary between the two approaches artificial and blurred.

anti_patterns.description: Believing AGI is imminent based solely on software and virtual AI progress.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Hardware and robotics (the physical components needed for real-world mastery) are not progressing at the same exponential rate as computing power. AGI requires physical embodiment.

anti_patterns.description: Accepting the 'AI Winter' as a global historical fact.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It was primarily an American funding phenomenon. Significant neural network and cybernetics research continued heavily in Europe and Japan (e.g., the Fifth Generation project) during the 1970s and 1980s.

anti_patterns.description: Crediting AlexNet with starting the deep learning revolution.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: DanNet (a similar deep CNN on GPUs) won four computer vision competitions before AlexNet existed. AlexNet's fame was largely due to Google's massive PR machine rather than being the first of its kind.
It is a 'stupid way' of planning that is computationally inefficient and unnatural. Humans do not plan this way; they decompose the future into meaningful chunks and higher-level sub-goals.

anti_patterns.description: Relying solely on Markovian situations for reinforcement learning.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Assuming the current input tells you everything you need to know is only good enough for board games. It fails in the real world where entities become hidden and memory is required to track them.
